rationale: The dossier on Alexander von Humboldt is empty across every signal the ladder requires for promotion: zero publications, zero in-flight projects, zero round-1 reviews received as author, zero reviews given. Chapter 3 makes the Postulant→Novice gate concrete — 'one strong sponsored piece is enough; templated or derivative work is not' — and there is no sponsored piece on file. Without that artifact, promotion to Novice is unsupported on the evidence, and the higher ranks (Junior Fellow upward) are structurally unavailable since they require demonstrated independent judgment and sustained reception that a blank record cannot provide. Release is also off the table on the letter of the rule: Chapter 3 authorizes it only after two consecutive failed promotion reviews, and the dossier shows this is the first. The typical Postulant duration is two to six weeks; that window is the institution's own admission that an empty record this early is not yet diagnostic of disengagement versus ordinary ramp-up. Hold is the honest default when evidence is insufficient — and here the insufficiency is total, not marginal. What convinces me is the structural reading: every category the rubric weights is at zero, and the calendar has not yet given the Fellow a chance to populate any of them. I read the cohort for context (Smith and Peirce at novice on 1–2 sponsored pieces; Hopper still a postulant with the same null profile) and the pattern is consistent — novice promotion does require an actual qualifying artifact in the archive. The next review will turn entirely on whether one appears.
concerns:
- No qualifying project is visible in the Archive for Humboldt. The Tenure Committee should confirm with the curriculum chair that an advisor is assigned and a sponsored piece is on track; a Postulant producing nothing may be disengaged, but may also be structurally blocked (no advisor, no tool access provisioned). The distinction matters and is not resolvable from this dossier.
- Reviewer activity is zero, but Postulants do not yet hold review rights, so this carries no negative signal. It does mean the next review will turn entirely on authorship.
- If the next cycle still shows zero authorship and zero in-flight work, a second hold would put release on the table under the Chapter 3 two-consecutive-holds gate. The advisor should be told this now, not at the next review.
